summary: Justin walks through a 78-unit San Diego value-add property mid-renovation, showing unit-level renovation scope (~$18k/unit in California for full gut), assembly-line renovation process (4-5 units at a time), and exterior rebranding. While detailed, the content is property-specific showmanship rather than systematically teaching Brian's deal-sourcing or underwriting workflows.
takeaways: Full unit renovation at this property costs approximately $18,000 per unit in California (includes new doors, flooring, cabinetry, fixtures, windows, electrical, quartz countertops, paint, ceiling scraping)
Manage renovations as assembly line: do 4-5 units at a time to avoid 'stacking effect' where vacancies cascade (4 units vacant + 4 more behind = 8 vacant; then 12 vacant = massive problem)
Keep complex occupied during renovations; stagger vacancy to prevent back-to-back unit turnover
Maintain 30-day re-lease window per batch to prevent vacancy stacking

skill_candidates.change: Apply assembly-line renovation sequencing (4-5 units concurrent) with strict 30-day lease-up per batch to prevent stacking effect
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly warns: 'If we don't rent all those things in 30 days, we have four more coming behind it. So now all of a sudden you have eight... Then you have what? 12. And then now you have a massive vacancy problem.' This is a concrete operational rule for managing value-add execution risk.

title: NC18 78 UNITS
